user: explain HOUSING DEVELOPMENT (CONTROL AND LICENSING) ACT 1966
bot:
The Housing Development (Control and Licensing) Act 1966 is a Malaysian law that regulates the housing development industry in Peninsular Malaysia. The Act aims to control and license housing developers to ensure that they operate in a fair and transparent manner, and to protect the interests of purchasers.

The Act requires housing developers to obtain a license from the Controller of Housing before they can engage in any housing development activities. The license is subject to certain conditions, including the payment of a deposit and the provision of prescribed documents.

The Act also imposes various duties on licensed housing developers, including the requirement to:

* Open and maintain a Housing Development Account with a bank or finance company
* Pay into the account all purchase moneys received from the sale of housing accommodation
* Not withdraw any money from the account except as authorized by regulations
* Keep and maintain proper accounting and other records
* Appoint an auditor to audit their accounts
* Furnish to the Controller written particulars of any alterations to documents submitted to the Controller
* Exhibit at all times in a conspicuous position in their office a copy of their license, advertisement and sale permit, and a copy of their last audited balance-sheet

The Act also provides for the investigation and enforcement of offenses under the Act, including the power to enter and search premises, seize documents and property, and examine persons.

The Act also establishes the Tribunal for Homebuyer Claims, which has jurisdiction to hear and determine claims by homebuyers against licensed housing developers.

Overall, the Housing Development (Control and Licensing) Act 1966 is an important piece of legislation that aims to regulate the housing development industry in Malaysia and protect the interests of purchasers.

This is not legal advice. Please consult a qualified lawyer.

user:
how the Arrangement or agreement affecting the business of a licensed
housing developer
bot:
According to Section 8 of the Housing Development (Control and Licensing) Act 1966, an arrangement or agreement affecting the business of a licensed housing developer is subject to the following conditions:

(1) A licensed housing developer who proposes to enter into an arrangement or agreement to sell, transfer, assign, dispose of, or reconstruct his business or management relating to housing development must notify the Controller as soon as possible.

(2) The licensed housing developer shall not proceed with the arrangement or agreement unless it is approved by the Controller.

(3) The Controller may approve the arrangement or agreement, or refuse to give approval, and may impose conditions on the approval for the purpose of carrying out the provisions of the Act.

In summary, any arrangement or agreement that affects the business of a licensed housing developer requires prior notification to the Controller and approval from the Controller before it can be implemented.

This is not legal advice. Please consult a qualified lawyer.

user: When does does a Purchaser take Vacant Possession
bot:
According to the Document Text, specifically in the section "When does a Purchaser take Vacant Possession?", it is stated that:

"The Court Held:-
It rejected the Developers contention.
It further held
[30]  In the considered opinion of this Court, the effect of the relevant sub-clauses 27(2) and 27(3) of the aforesaid Schedule H sale and purchase agreement on delivery of vacant possession is that when a post-completion Notice of Delivery of Vacant Possession has been issued by the developer to the purchasers, a purchaser who takes the keys and vacant possession before the expiry of the 30 days thereafter is regarded as having taken delivery of the vacant possession on the day he/she takes actual vacant possession, while another purchaser who takes the keys and vacant possession after the expiry of the 30 day period is deemed to have taken vacant possession on the last day of the 30 days period."

This is based on the case of Lawrence Lim Cheng Poh & Ors v Wealh Plateau Sdn Bhd [2022] MLJU 3390.

This is not legal advice. Please consult a qualified lawyer.

The Defect Liability Period (DLP) refers to the period of time during which a developer is responsible for rectifying any defects in a property. According to the provided document, the DLP is 24 months after the date the purchaser takes vacant possession of the property. Any defect, shrinkage, or other faults that become apparent within this period and are due to defective workmanship or materials, or non-compliance with approved plans and descriptions, must be repaired and made good by the developer at its own cost and expense within 30 days of receiving written notice from the purchaser. 

This is not legal advice. Please consult a qualified lawyer.

user: limitation of action to claim damage
bot:
According to the provided Document Text, under the Limitation Act 1953, there is a limitation of action to claim damages. Specifically, Section 6 of the Limitation Act 1953 is mentioned, but the details of the section are not provided in the text. However, it is mentioned that for latent defects, the "starting date" and "knowledge required for bringing an action" are relevant factors in determining the limitation period.

Additionally, it is stated that the defect liability clause does not take away the right to sue in respect of defects which were not discoverable, and that the provision of an express remedy for making good the defects does not take away the rights of the purchaser which follow at common law in a case of breach of contract (Fong Wan Realty Sdn Bhd v PJ Condominium Sdn Bhd [2009] MLJU 1428).

It is also mentioned that there is no bar to pursuing an action in tort for negligence even after the expiry of the DLP, provided it is brought within 6 years from the date the cause of action arose (The Ara Joint Management Body v Mammoth Land & Development Sdn Bhd [2017] MLJU).

This is not legal advice. Please consult a qualified lawyer.

user: what are the court have recognised
bot:
The court has recognised the following industry standards:

1. UBBL- Minimum Standards 
2. SPAN- Water proof
3. BSI- Concrete
4. CIDB- Qlassic -CIS 7.

These standards have been referenced in various court cases, including:
- KC CHAN BROTHERS DEVELOPMENT SDN BHD v TAN KON SENG & ORS [2001] 6 MLJ 636 (“KC CHAN”)
- Badan Pengurusan Bersama Tropicana Bay Residence @ Penang World City v Mutiara Metropolis Sdn Bhd & Ors [2024] MLJU 1948
- Helena a/p K. Ghanamuthu v Fabulous Range Sdn. Bhd. [2019] MLJU 1601- CIS7 (CIDB Standards)
